prompt: Pharmaceutical vial product photography. The reference image is the locked target style and quality. Reproduce the reference identically: same clear glass 3ml vial geometry and proportions, same navy blue aluminum collar cap with matching navy blue flip-off button, same pure white seamless studio background with soft even lighting and the subtle shadow beneath the base, same matte-white pharmaceutical label wrapped around the lower two-thirds of the body, same upper-third blank pristine white logo zone (absolutely no graphics, no marker boxes, no dashed lines, no placeholder symbols), same thin navy blue hairline rule above and below the product name, same slim solid navy blue accent bar running along the very bottom edge of the label, same vial composition and framing within the 4:5 portrait. Critical typography requirement: the product name on the label MUST use the EXACT same typography as the reference — same medium-bold geometric sans-serif typeface, same letter weight (medium-to-bold, NOT thin, NOT light, NOT regular — match the substantial visual weight of the reference 'BPC-157' text exactly), same charcoal grey color, same letter-spacing, same proportional size relative to the label width, same letter aspect ratio (letters have normal natural proportions — NOT vertically stretched or elongated, NOT condensed or narrow, the height-to-width ratio of each letter matches the reference 'BPC-157' exactly). The new product name simply replaces the old text within that exact same typographic style and weight. The ONLY change versus the reference: the product name text now reads 'BPC-157 / TB500' instead of 'BPC-157'. Keep every other element pixel-faithful to the reference. Sharp focus, high resolution, premium pharmaceutical product shot.
